relation.rationale: Node A asserts that market spillover has low prior probability (markets were not common SARS1 spillover locations, so zoonosis shouldn't receive high Bayes factor for HSM cluster), while Node B asserts that zoonotic spillover is statistically more common as a cause of pandemics overall—they directly conflict on whether zoonotic origin should receive high evidential weight when predicting wet market involvement. (shared question: Should the zoonotic origin hypothesis receive high Bayesian support from wet market epidemiology and prior pandemic statistics?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The Huanan Seafood Market epicenter constitutes a major factor weighing against lab leak hypothesis.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: As you can see from my analysis, the major factors weighing against LL were the probability that WIV could carry out DEFUSE style work, and the epicenter being HSM.

relation.rationale: Scott Alexander argues the HSM cluster should NOT receive high evidential weight for zoonosis (because markets were not common SARS1 spillover sites), while Judge Will asserts the HSM epicenter IS a major factor weighing against lab leak—making it strong evidence for zoonosis. Both authors directly assert opposing conclusions about whether the HSM cluster provides strong support for the natural origin hypothesis. (shared question: Does the Huanan Seafood Market cluster constitute strong evidence supporting the zoonotic origin hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: Seafood markets are not uniquely coincidental as early cluster locations because they repeatedly formed early clusters across multiple countries during zero-COVID periods.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
As this is the short version, here is the simplest way to see it: HSM is not such a coincidence since seafood markets and facilities repeatedly form early clusters.
2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020, as well as outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore, started in markets. 
Most notable among them is the December 2020 outbreak in Thailand, following a long period of zero Covid (i.e.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that markets were not frequently involved in SARS1 spillover and therefore should not receive high Bayes factor credit for the HSM cluster; Node B asserts that seafood markets repeatedly formed early clusters across multiple countries and therefore the HSM cluster is not a surprising coincidence—they directly oppose whether market involvement in early clusters is rare or common. (shared question: Should the Huanan seafood market cluster count as strong evidence for the zoonotic origin hypothesis, or is market involvement in early outbreaks too common to confer significant Bayesian support?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The lab-leak origin hypothesis for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than natural zoonotic origin based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: The author defends the thesis that a lab-leak origin for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than a natural zoonotic origin, based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: The author defends the thesis that a lab-leak origin for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than a natural zoonotic origin, based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start, and argues this reassessment warrants serious attention despite political polarization.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that markets were not a common spillover location, therefore zoonosis should not receive high evidential weight from the HSM cluster; Node B asserts lab-leak origin is now more probable than zoonosis based on accumulated evidence—directly opposing conclusions about which origin hypothesis the evidence supports. (shared question: Does the Huanan seafood market cluster provide strong evidence for the zoonotic origin hypothesis of SARS-CoV-2?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The location of the outbreak epicenter at Huanan Seafood Market provides major evidence against lab leak hypothesis.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: I think under the LL hypothesis, HSM was not a likely place for emergence.

relation.rationale: Scott Alexander asserts that markets should NOT receive high Bayes factor support from the HSM cluster (because markets weren't common SARS1 spillover sites), while Judge Will asserts that the HSM epicenter IS major evidence against lab leak (implicitly supporting zoonosis). They directly disagree on whether the market cluster location provides strong evidential support for the zoonotic origin hypothesis. (shared question: Does the Huanan Seafood Market cluster location provide strong evidence for the zoonotic origin hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The hypothesis that many other seafood markets also had initial clusters like HSM has less than 1% probability of being true.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: I understand this claim so deeply that I attribute less than 1% chance of it being true. (Otherwise, that becomes a superior explanation, at 1% * 1%) Furthermore, I analyzed all other initial clusters in seafood markets and facilities in China, Thailand, and Singapore.

relation.rationale: Node A argues that markets were not common SARS1 spillover locations, so the HSM cluster should not receive high Bayesian support for zoonosis; Node B counters that alternative seafood market clusters are extremely unlikely (<1%), implicitly defending the HSM cluster as distinctive evidence for zoonotic spillover—they disagree on whether the market location pattern supports the zoonosis hypothesis. (shared question: Does the Huanan seafood market cluster pattern provide strong evidential support for the zoonotic origin hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The absence of definitive wildlife spillover evidence at HSM despite extensive searching further diminishes the evidential weight of the HSM cluster.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: Last, the remarkable lack of evidence for a wildlife spillover in HSM, despite an extensive search, further reduces this factor.

relation.rationale: Scott Alexander argues that the market cluster should not receive high evidential weight for zoonosis because markets were not frequent spillover sites historically, while the response author argues the HSM cluster's evidential weight is further diminished by the absence of wildlife spillover evidence found despite extensive searching—they directly contradict on whether the HSM cluster provides meaningful support for the zoonotic origin hypothesis. (shared question: Does the Huanan Seafood Market cluster and the lack of definitive wildlife spillover evidence at HSM constitute strong evidence for or against the zoonotic origin hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The conditional probability P(Wuhan|ZWM) for a zoonotic market spillover occurring specifically in Wuhan is much less than 1/100, likely around 1/1000.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: For the market branch of the ZW hypothesis, ZWM, the likelihood drops even more since it has a much smaller fraction of the wildlife trade than of the population. The total mammalian trade in all the Wuhan markets was running under 10,000 animals/year.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: Advanced note: This analysis is a bit simplified as it doesn’t take into account that Wuhan has much less of these interfaces than other places in China (a city doesn’t have many farms, and wildlife consumption is not popular outside south China).

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: ZO argues for up to 5% based on some claims about rail lines and population density that are hard to evaluate.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: Wuhan's share of the ZWM risk is not likely to be more than 1/1000, distinctly less than the population share of 1/100.

relation.rationale: Scott Alexander argues that markets should NOT receive a high Bayes factor for zoonosis because they were not common SARS1 spillover locations, while Judge Will's node asserts a specific low conditional probability P(Wuhan|ZWM) ≈ 1/1000 that presupposes markets ARE a relevant spillover pathway to evaluate—they disagree on whether market-based spillover is sufficiently predictive by the zoonosis hypothesis. (shared question: Does the zoonotic market spillover hypothesis deserve significant evidential weight (high Bayes factor / meaningful conditional probability) given historical spillover patterns and wildlife trade data?)

contradicted_by.canonical: Rootclaim's overall Bayesian analysis assigns the HSM early cluster only 2x evidential weight for zoonosis.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: The HSM early cluster is therefore negligible as evidence. Our analysis assigns it 2x.

relation.rationale: Scott Alexander argues the HSM cluster should NOT receive high Bayes factor weight for zoonosis because markets weren't common SARS1 spillover sites, while Rootclaim responds by asserting they DO assign it 2x weight—disagreeing on whether the HSM cluster provides meaningful evidential support for zoonosis. (shared question: Should the Huanan seafood market (HSM) early cluster carry significant evidential weight in favor of the zoonotic origin hypothesis?)

supported_by.canonical: The hypothesis that many other seafood markets also had initial clusters like HSM has less than 1% probability of being true.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: I understand this claim so deeply that I attribute less than 1% chance of it being true. (Otherwise, that becomes a superior explanation, at 1% * 1%) Furthermore, I analyzed all other initial clusters in seafood markets and facilities in China, Thailand, and Singapore.

relation.rationale: The argument that markets weren't common SARS1 spillover locations supports the low probability (<1%) that multiple seafood markets had initial clusters, as it challenges the specialness of HSM.

nodes.canonical: A British expatriate Connor Reed reported getting COVID symptoms in November, three weeks before the first Huanan wet market case, with later hospital testing confirming COVID.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: A British expatriate in Wuhan, Connor Reed, says he got sick in November, three weeks before the first wet market case. Later the hospital tested his samples and said it was COVID.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: A British expatriate in Wuhan, Connor Reed, says he got sick in November, three weeks before the first wet market case. Later the hospital tested his samples and said it was Covid. Another paper reports 90 cases before the first wet market one.

nodes.canonical: A published paper reports 90 COVID cases occurring before the first Huanan wet market case.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: Another paper reports 90 cases before the first wet market one.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: Another paper reports 90 cases before the first wet market one.

contradicted_by.canonical: The apparent emergence of SARS-CoV-2 at Huanan Seafood Market rather than other Wuhan locations constitutes evidence supporting zoonotic origin

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: Factor supported ZO: Apparent emergence of SARS-CoV-2 at the Huanan Seafood Market vs. other places in Wuhan

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that 90 cases occurred before the first wet market case (undermining market-origin hypothesis), while Node B asserts that the apparent emergence at Huanan Seafood Market supports zoonotic origin—these positions are incompatible on whether the HSM cluster pattern evidences zoonotic spillover. (shared question: Does the temporal and spatial clustering of early COVID cases at Huanan Seafood Market constitute evidence for zoonotic origin?)

contradicted_by.canonical: Baidu symptom search data from Wuhan shows minimal signal for coronavirus-like symptoms before December 2019

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote:
ZO debate 1, slide 116 - Wuhan, Baidu data shows very little signal for symptoms of a circulating
coronavirus prior to December
ZO debate 1, slide 114 - Wuhan, 3850 blood bank samples from Sep-Dec 2019 show no positives
for antibodies
ZO debate 1, slide 113 - Wuhan, 640 throat swabs Oct 6th - Jan 21st 2020.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that 90 COVID cases occurred before the first Huanan wet market case, while Node B presents evidence (Baidu searches, blood bank samples, throat swabs) showing minimal/no coronavirus signal in Wuhan before December 2019, directly contradicting the claim that cases preceded market detection. (shared question: Did COVID-19 cases circulate in Wuhan before the Huanan wet market cluster was identified?)

nodes.canonical: The alleged patient's claim that his cat also died of coronavirus is scientifically implausible, casting doubt on the entire testimony.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: He also told the Mail that his cat died of coronavirus too, which is rare-to-impossible.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: He also told the Mail that his cat got the coronavirus too, which is impossible.

nodes.canonical: The demographic implausibility of a 25-year-old seeking hospital testing contradicts the claimed positive COVID test, since young adults rarely required hospitalization for coronavirus.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: Also, to get a positive hospital test, he would have had to go to the hospital, but he was 25 years old and almost no 25-year-olds go to the hospital for coronavirus.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: Also, to get a positive hospital test, he would have had to go to the hospital, but he was 25 years old and almost no 25-year-olds go to the hospital for coronavirus.

nodes.canonical: Both judges independently ruled in favor of Peter and the zoonosis hypothesis, representing a decisive rather than marginal victory.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: Peter and the zoonosis hypothesis. This was a decisive victory. There were two judges, who each gave separate verdicts (or were allowed to declare a draw). Both judges decided in favor of Peter.

supported_by.canonical: SARS-CoV-2 is substantially more likely to have zoonotic origin than to result from intentional engineering or lab leak.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: I find that SARS-CoV-2 is substantially more likely to be of zoonotic origin than a product of intentional engineering.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote:
Ultimately, this means my decision in
this debate is somewhere between "I think SARS-CoV-2 more likely than not had a natural origin" and
"Based on the data presented by each of the sides in this debate, and ~70 hours of time spent evaluating
and synthesizing those arguments/evidence, I think SARS-CoV-2 more likely than not had a natural
origin".

attestations.quote: I find with high confidence that zoonotic spillover is the more likely origin

Lack of evidence of circulating virus prior to HSM superspreading event, implying early HSM infections

I think ZO won this part of the debate cleanly. Below is a summary of the data presented by ZO showing
that SARS-CoV-2 couldn’t have been circulating prior to the HSM. The two most convincing pieces of
evidence for me were the throat swabs of influenza-like-illness (ILI) patients and routine blood draws.

relation.rationale: Scott Alexander's dramatic credence shift from 50-50 to 90-10 favoring zoonosis after observing the debate provides independent corroboration of the zoonotic origin conclusion.

nodes.canonical: Peter Miller's reasoning for the 1/10,000 estimate was based on the proportion of Wuhan citizens working at the wet market, treating the market as one random location among many where a lab leak could appear.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: Peter's argument went something like: obviously a zoonotic pandemic would start at a site selling weird animals. But a lab leak pandemic - if it didn't start at the lab - could show up anywhere. 1/10,000 Wuhan citizens work at the wet market. So if a lab leak was going to show up somewhere random, the wet market was a 1/10,000 chance

contradicted_by.canonical: The calculation of p(HSM|Lab Leak,Wuhan) as 0.01-0.1% is justified by reference to the first officially confirmed case being an HSM shrimp vendor, one of 1000 HSM vendors, out of 10 million Wuhan residents.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
p(HSM|Lab Leak,Wuhan) – The mistake everyone makes here is thinking this is a 0.1% to 0.01% coincidence, usually justified by the first officially confirmed case being an HSM shrimp vendor, one of 1000 HSM vendors, out of 10 million Wuhan residents.
We use three independent methods to estimate this probability more accurately, all pointing to this number being larger than 1%.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts Peter Miller's calculation treats the HSM market as one random location among many (implying low prior probability for lab leak), while Node B directly challenges this reasoning by rejecting the 0.1-0.01% coincidence calculation and asserting three independent methods support a different estimate of p(HSM|Lab Leak,Wuhan). (shared question: What is the appropriate probability of observing the HSM cluster epicenter given a lab leak origin in Wuhan, and is the calculation based on treating the market as a random location among 10 million residents valid?)

nodes.canonical: The evidence does not better support the zoonosis hypothesis over lab leak when properly analyzed using Bayesian methods.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: Zoonosis is a more likely hypothesis due to being better supported by the evidence –  This is completely untrue, but to fully understand it one has to commit to learning how to do probabilistic inference correctly, which Scott could not free enough time to do.

contradicted_by.canonical: Zoonotic origin is more likely than lab leak based on key factors evaluated in the debate

contradicted_by.canonical: The lab leak side's calculation of prior probability based on confirmed and suspected lab leaks is a sound methodological approach in principle.

attestations.quote: One of the ways LL calculates the prior probability of a lab leak is by considering confirmed and suspected lab leaks. This seems like a good method.

(shared question: Does the available evidence and proper probabilistic analysis support the zoonotic origin hypothesis over the lab leak hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The prior probability of lab leak and zoonotic origin hypotheses should be assessed as initially comparable in the specific context of a Wuhan pandemic start due to concentrated suspect lab work there.

contradicted_by.canonical: Zoonotic spillover is statistically more common as a cause of pandemics than laboratory leaks when examining prior probabilities.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that evidence does not better support zoonosis over lab leak when properly analyzed, while Node B asserts that Peter Miller concluded with 99% confidence in zoonotic origin after investigation, directly contradicting on whether the evidence supports zoonosis. (shared question: Does the available evidence support the zoonotic origin hypothesis over the lab leak hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: Peter Miller's zoonosis case depends critically on the improbability that a lab leak pandemic would first appear at a wet market location

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: Saar: Peter's case hinges on the idea that it's very improbable that a lab leak pandemic would first show up at a wet market.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that Peter Miller's case depends on the improbability of a lab leak showing up at a wet market; Node B asserts that zoonosis is NOT better supported by evidence when properly analyzed, directly opposing Miller's evidentiary reasoning. (shared question: Does the evidence better support the zoonosis hypothesis (with HSM cluster as key evidence) compared to the lab leak hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The combined Bayesian analysis yields point estimate odds of approximately 12:1 favoring lab leak, which drop to about 4:1 when uncertainty in priors is incorporated.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote:
At this point of the analysis the combined point estimate of the logit is ~2.5 which would give odds about 12/1 favoring lab leak. When we consider how uncertain that estimate is, averaging over the range of reasonable priors, the odds would drop to about 4/1. Does that agree with other ballpark estimates?  

The lead author of Proximal Origins, Andersen, wrote his colleagues on 2/2/2020  “Natural selection and accidental release are both plausible scenarios explaining the data - and a priori should be equally weighed as possible explanations.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that Bayesian evidence does NOT better support zoonosis over lab leak; Node B asserts specific odds favoring lab leak (12:1 to 4:1), implying the evidence supports lab leak—they directly oppose each other on whether the Bayesian analysis favors zoonosis or lab leak. (shared question: Does Bayesian analysis of the evidence support the zoonosis hypothesis over the lab leak hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The lab-leak origin hypothesis for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than natural zoonotic origin based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: The author defends the thesis that a lab-leak origin for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than a natural zoonotic origin, based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: The author defends the thesis that a lab-leak origin for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than a natural zoonotic origin, based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start, and argues this reassessment warrants serious attention despite political polarization.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that evidence does not better support zoonosis over lab leak, while Node B asserts that lab-leak origin has become more probable than zoonotic origin based on accumulated evidence—both authors are making opposing claims about which hypothesis the evidence supports. (shared question: Does the accumulated evidence support lab-leak origin or zoonotic origin as more probable for SARS-CoV-2?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The likelihood of a lab leak would have been substantially higher if the Wuhan Institute of Virology had been conducting research in an irresponsible manner.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote:
But because WIV was researching such an infectious virus in such an irresponsible way, the odds of a leak were much higher.

The most likely explanation for all these facts is that WIV went ahead and did the gain-of-function research they said they were going to do (the particular DEFUSE grant proposal we know about got rejected, but it proves that Wuhan wanted to do this, and they could easily have gotten funding somewhere else, or done it out of their regular budget).

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that irresponsible WIV research practices substantially increased lab leak odds, while Node B asserts that zoonosis is more likely when evidence is properly analyzed using Bayesian methods—these represent opposing conclusions about whether lab leak or zoonosis is the better-supported hypothesis. (shared question: Is lab leak or zoonotic origin better supported by the evidence when using proper probabilistic reasoning?)

contradicted_by.canonical: Peter Miller expected a smart and unbiased person would assign approximately 80% probability to zoonosis after seeing all debate evidence

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: I [expected] a smart and unbiased person would vote for zoonosis with, say, 80% odds after seeing all the evidence.

relation.rationale: Node A reports Miller's expectation that evidence supports zoonosis at ~80% probability, while Node B (from the response) directly asserts this conclusion is 'completely untrue' and claims the evidence does not better support zoonosis when properly analyzed—directly opposing the evidentiary assessment underlying Miller's probability claim. (shared question: Does the debate evidence support the zoonosis hypothesis over the lab leak hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The annual probability of zoonotic pathogen emergence (approximately 1/3) exceeds the probability of research-incident emergence, justifying zoonosis as the default null hypothesis for generic new pathogens.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: That's higher than the probability of some widespread disease emerging from a research incident in any year, justifying the general view that if one must choose a favored "null hypothesis" for a generic new pathogen the choice would be ZW.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that Bayesian analysis does not support zoonosis over lab leak, while Node B asserts that zoonosis should be favored as the default null hypothesis based on empirical probability of natural emergence exceeding research-incident probability. (shared question: Does the available evidence and probabilistic reasoning support zoonosis as more likely than lab leak as the origin of SARS-CoV-2?)

contradicted_by.canonical: Although zoonotic origins have historically been more common for pandemics, the location of SARS-CoV-2's emergence in Wuhan—where suspect lab work was concentrated—made lab leak and zoonotic origins initially comparable in prior probability.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: Although zoonotic origins have been more common historically, the start in Wuhan where the suspect lab work was concentrated left the two possibilities with comparable probabilities.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that evidence does not better support zoonosis over lab leak when properly analyzed, while Node B asserts that Wuhan's location made lab leak and zoonosis initially comparable in prior probability—implying zoonosis became more likely after evidence analysis, directly opposing A's Bayesian conclusion. (shared question: Does the totality of evidence (particularly Wuhan location and lab capabilities) support zoonotic origin more strongly than lab leak origin?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The reassessment of pandemic origins toward lab-leak probability warrants serious attention despite existing political polarization on the issue.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: a lab-leak origin for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than a natural zoonotic origin, based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start, and argues this reassessment warrants serious attention despite political polarization

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that evidence does not better support zoonosis over lab leak when properly analyzed, while Node B asserts that lab-leak origin has become more probable than zoonotic origin based on accumulated evidence—these are directly opposing conclusions about which hypothesis is better supported by evidence. (shared question: Does the accumulated evidence better support the lab-leak hypothesis or the zoonotic-origin hypothesis for SARS-CoV-2?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The epidemiological proximity of earliest COVID indicators to plausible animal sources rather than laboratory sources is the most important basis for the zoonotic conclusion

contradicted_by.canonical: The reassessment of COVID origins toward lab-leak probability warrants serious attention despite political polarization surrounding the issue.

attestations.quote:
The zoonosis hypothesis fully depends on the claim that it is an extreme coincidence that the early Covid patients were in HSM – a market with wildlife – unless a zoonotic spillover occurred there. 
The rest of the evidence strongly supports the lab-leak hypothesis, so if this claim is mistaken, lab-leak becomes the most likely hypothesis.
There are multiple cases where a country has had zero Covid cases for a while, and then a cluster of cases appears in a seafood market.

Scott prepared a very useful table comparing the probabilities various people gave to the evidence about Covid origins (discussed later in more details). It nicely shows how the zoonosis conclusion stands on this single leg, and once it is removed, lab-leak becomes the winning hypothesis (Specifically, Scott will flip to 94% lab-leak).

(shared question: Does the epidemiological and geographic evidence favor zoonotic origin over lab leak origin for SARS-CoV-2?)

(shared question: Did SARS-CoV-2 originate from natural zoonotic spillover or from a laboratory leak?)

contradicted_by.canonical: The author concludes that Wuhan was not an unusually distinctive location for the purposes of evaluating where a pandemic might emerge.

attestations.quote: In summary, I think Wuhan was a reasonably unl

attestations.author: Rootclaim

I think Wuhan's a fairly likely place for a pandemic to start.

nodes.canonical: The calculation of p(HSM|Lab Leak,Wuhan) as 0.01-0.1% is justified by reference to the first officially confirmed case being an HSM shrimp vendor, one of 1000 HSM vendors, out of 10 million Wuhan residents.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
p(HSM|Lab Leak,Wuhan) – The mistake everyone makes here is thinking this is a 0.1% to 0.01% coincidence, usually justified by the first officially confirmed case being an HSM shrimp vendor, one of 1000 HSM vendors, out of 10 million Wuhan residents.
We use three independent methods to estimate this probability more accurately, all pointing to this number being larger than 1%.

contradicted_by.canonical: Peter Miller's reasoning for the 1/10,000 estimate was based on the proportion of Wuhan citizens working at the wet market, treating the market as one random location among many where a lab leak could appear.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: Peter's argument went something like: obviously a zoonotic pandemic would start at a site selling weird animals. But a lab leak pandemic - if it didn't start at the lab - could show up anywhere. 1/10,000 Wuhan citizens work at the wet market. So if a lab leak was going to show up somewhere random, the wet market was a 1/10,000 chance

(shared question: What is the appropriate probability of observing the HSM cluster epicenter given a lab leak origin in Wuhan, and is the calculation based on treating the market as a random location among 10 million residents valid?)

nodes.canonical: Two out of five large COVID-19 outbreaks in China during 2020 originated in markets, along with outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore seafood markets.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: 2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020, as well as outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore, started in markets.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
Contrary to this claim, an empirical analysis reveals that seafood markets and facilities repeatedly formed initial Covid clusters following a period of zero infections. This was observed in 2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020 (Xinfadi and Dalian), as well as in outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore:

For the Xinfadi outbreak in June 2020: among the 368 persons isolated and treated, 272 (73.9%) had epidemiologic links to the market—169 (46%) were market workers and 103 (28%) were visitors.

attestations.quote: Peter: The first officially confirmed COVID case was a vendor at the Wuhan wet market. So were the next four, and half of the next 40. At this point, what was claimed to be 10,000x evidence (increases the odds of the zoonosis hypothesis by 10,000), turns out to be less than 5x – Because, as explained in 1, we need to divide the two conditional probabilities, which are at best 5% and 1%, giving a ratio lower than 5x.

The zoonosis hypothesis fully depends on the claim that it is an extreme coincidence that the early Covid patients were in HSM – a market with wildlife – unless a zoonotic spillover occurred there. 
The rest of the evidence strongly supports the lab-leak hypothesis, so if this claim is mistaken, lab-leak becomes the most likely hypothesis.
There are multiple cases where a country has had zero Covid cases for a while, and then a cluster of cases appears in a seafood market.

nodes.canonical: Extensive searches at HSM failed to find evidence of wildlife spillover, which further weakens the evidential value of the cluster.

Awwww, come on, you can’t stay mad at this little guy.

Scientists were finally able to confirm the existence of the raccoon-dog shop in the funniest possible way: a virologist had visited Wuhan in 2014, saw the awful conditions in the shop, and took a picture as an example of the kind of place that a future pandemic might start.

relation.rationale: The failure to find wildlife spillover evidence at HSM despite extensive searching weakens the significance of raccoon-dogs being present at the market.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: Instead of explaining the whole methodology and how it applies to Covid origins, which will take too long, we will focus on the main mistake in all the analyses in Scott’s post – believing that the early cluster of cases in the Huanan Seafood Market (HSM) is strong evidence for zoonosis. Scott prepared a very useful table comparing the probabilities various people gave to the evidence about Covid origins (discussed later in more details). It nicely shows how the zoonosis conclusion stands on this single leg, and once it is removed, lab-leak becomes the winning hypothesis (Specifically, Scott will flip to 94% lab-leak).

relation.rationale: Rootclaim's overall assignment of only 2x evidential weight to the HSM cluster directly supports the claim that HSM provides negligible evidence for zoonosis.

His only evidence that it was COVID was that two months later, the hospital supposedly “notified” him that it was. The hospital never inform

contradicted_by.canonical: A COVID-19 case detected during the early period (likely pre-lockdown) was only found because the patient was a relative of a hospital worker who recognized symptoms and insisted on hospital visit despite absence of wet market connection.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote:
One of the few non-market-connected COVID cases detected during this period was only detected because he was the relative of a hospital worker; the worker noticed the signs and insisted they go to the hospital despite the lack of a wet market connection.

Although the map of positive samples and cases at the market was centered near the raccoon-dog stall, that could be because that area was sampled more; it’s also close to the mahjong room, where visitors and vendors at the market would go and unwind in a tight, poorly ventilated area.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that a non-market-connected case was detected due to special circumstances (hospital worker intervention), implying market-connected cases were preferentially detected; Node B asserts that early isolated cases have inherently low detection probability regardless of market connection, contradicting the implication that market connection alone explains detection patterns. (shared question: Does the detection of early COVID-19 cases depend primarily on market connection, or on other factors like testing access and symptom recognition?)

4.4 Early ascertainment bias
The ascertainment rate of covid refers to the fraction of covid cases that become known;
before covid became known, the main way for people to be diagnosed is retrospectively in
samples that were collected while they were in a hospital.

relation.rationale: Low detection probability for early isolated cases due to lack of symptoms or testing provides a mechanistic basis for why ascertainment bias could concentrate detected cases at locations with heightened surveillance.

attestations.quote: LL argued that HSM had several properties that would make it the most likely place for the first superspreading event to be ascertained including: the limited ventilation

relation.rationale: Node A (Rootclaim's own assertion) claims the HSM cluster is negligible evidence against lab leak; Node B (Judge Will's own assertion) claims lab leak proponents argued HSM's properties made it the most likely location for detecting a superspreading event—these represent opposing conclusions about HSM's evidential significance for the lab leak hypothesis. (shared question: Does the HSM cluster's characteristics constitute meaningful evidence against or in favor of the lab leak origin hypothesis?)

nodes.canonical: Each calculation method provides a minimum estimate for p(HSM|Lab Leak,Wuhan) that individually exceeds 1%.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: We provide three methods to estimate this number, all of them supporting a conclusion that this conditional probability is over 1%. Given that each of these provides a minimum estimate, and some are independent alternative explanations for an HSM cluster, a reasonable final estimate would be 5-10%.

contradicted_by.canonical: Final posterior odds ratio of lab leak to zoonosis is approximately 3.6E-3 (roughly 1 in 300), representing combined effect of prior and evidential updates.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: The final, then is P0(LL) / P0(ZO) * P(LL) / P(ZO) = 3.6E-3

relation.rationale: Node A (Rootclaim/Saar) asserts HSM cluster is consistent with lab leak (p(HSM|LL) = 5-10%), while Node B (Judge Will) treats the HSM epicenter as a decisive factor supporting zoonosis over lab leak (posterior odds 1:300 against lab leak), representing fundamentally opposing conclusions about what the HSM cluster evidence demonstrates. (shared question: Does the HSM cluster concentration constitute strong evidence favoring zoonotic origin over lab leak origin?)

nodes.canonical: The conditional probability p(HSM|Lab Leak,Wuhan) must account for HSM's environmental features that make it prone to early cluster formation regardless of origin mechanism

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
Method 1 – Factors in HSM
HSM is not some random location in Wuhan, and it has a number of environmental factors that significantly increase the likelihood of forming an early Covid cluster compared to other busy locations:

contradicted_by.canonical: Under the lab leak hypothesis, HSM was not a likely place for the outbreak to emerge.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: I think under the LL hypothesis, HSM was not a likely place for emergence.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that HSM's environmental features make it likely to form an early cluster regardless of origin, implying high p(HSM|Lab Leak), while Node B asserts that under lab leak hypothesis HSM was not a likely place for emergence, directly contradicting Node A's claim about HSM probability under LL. (shared question: Under the lab leak hypothesis, how likely was HSM to be the location where the outbreak emerged?)

nodes.canonical: Comparative probability tables are used to demonstrate the conditional probability comparison between zoonosis and lab leak given HSM cluster.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: uses comparative probability tables and cross-domain examples of seafood market outbreak clusters

supported_by.canonical: Demaneuf and De Maistre's analysis estimated lab-related to non-lab-related odds for a Wuhan outbreak at approximately 1:1 conservatively and 4:1 for their base estimate.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: Demaneuf and De Maistre looked in detail at past evidence for various scenarios of natural and lab-related outbreaks. They consider ZL accounts, but the factors that go into whether there's a research leak are largely the same as for LL, with the difference arising more from factors we have not yet included. Without considering sequence features beyond that the virus is SARS-related they conservatively estimate that the lab-related to non-lab-related odds for an outbreak in Wuhan, P(ZL|Wuhan)/P(ZW|Wuhan), are about one-to-one. Their base estimate, for which they make no special effort to lean either way, is about 4/1.

relation.rationale: The use of comparative probability tables as analytical methodology supports the quantitative Bayesian likelihood ratio approach employed by Demaneuf and De Maistre.

nodes.canonical: Worobey et al. conducted a famous paper attempting to analyze early COVID-19 case clusters

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: The famous paper by Worobey et al. attempted a similar analysis and concluded that 1676 sites in Wuhan were superior to HSM in terms of superspreading potential.

contradicted_by.canonical: The reassessment of pandemic origins toward lab-leak probability warrants serious attention despite existing political polarization on the issue.

attestations.author: Michael Weissman
attestations.quote: a lab-leak origin for SARS-CoV-2 has become more probable than a natural zoonotic origin, based on accumulated evidence since the pandemic's start, and argues this reassessment warrants serious attention despite political polarization

relation.rationale: Node A (Rootclaim/Saar position) asserts that Worobey et al.'s finding of 1676 superior sites undermines the HSM cluster as evidence for market origin, implicitly supporting lab-leak; Node B asserts that accumulated evidence now makes lab-leak origin MORE PROBABLE than zoonotic origin. However, these represent opposing positions on the same underlying question: whether the evidence supports lab-leak or zoonotic origin as the more probable explanation. (shared question: Does the available evidence (including analysis of early case clusters and HSM epicenter) support lab-leak or zoonotic origin as the more probable explanation for SARS-CoV-2?)

nodes.canonical: A study claiming 1676 Wuhan sites were superior to HSM in superspreading potential relied on false assumptions and failed to identify any truly superior location.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
attempted a similar analysis and concluded that 1676 sites in Wuhan were superior to HSM in terms of superspreading potential. We looked at their data in detail and found that it was based on several false assumptions. In fact, given the above requirements, we couldn’t identify a single location in their list that was superior to HSM.
Large hospitals are the only plausible locations, as they have many permanent residents (staff and patients), and are likely to receive early cases.

nodes.canonical: The Xinfadi seafood market outbreak in June 2020 had 73.9% of cases epidemiologically linked to the market, with the seafood market section having the most infections.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
For the Xinfadi outbreak in June 2020: among the 368 persons isolated and treated, 272 (73.9%) had epidemiologic links to the market—169 (46%) were market workers and 103 (28%) were visitors. All the remaining 96 (26%) were close contacts of the other cases. More specifically, the seafood market within Xinfadi had the most infections.
Incidentally, this is markedly different from HSM, where most of the early cases could not be connected to it.
For the Dalian outbreak in July 2020, the outbreak was in Dalian Kaiyang World Seafood, a major seafood processing facility.

contradicted_by.canonical: The lack of evidence of circulating virus prior to the HSM superspreading event implies early HSM infections contained or were close to the index case, supporting zoonotic origin.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: Factor supported ZO: Lack of evidence of circulating virus prior to HSM superspreading event, implying early HSM infections contained (or were close) to the index case

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that most early HSM cases could NOT be connected to the market, while Node B asserts that the lack of evidence of circulating virus prior to HSM implies early infections were contained/close to the index case—these draw opposite conclusions about what the early HSM case distribution reveals about market origin. (shared question: Does the pattern of early HSM cases (their connection or lack thereof to the market location) support or undermine the zoonotic origin hypothesis?)

contradicted_by.canonical: Wei Guixian, an HSM seafood vendor, was the index case or first ascertained case, infected on December 10th, 2019.

attestations.author: Judge Will
attestations.quote: At the HSM, the animals infects the index case (or at least first ascertained case): an HSM seafood vendor named Wei Guixian, on December 10th, 2019.

relation.rationale: Node A asserts that most early HSM cases could NOT be connected to the market, while Node B asserts that Wei Guixian, an HSM seafood vendor, was the index case infected at the market on December 10th—directly contradicting the claim that early cases lacked market connection. (shared question: Were the early HSM COVID-19 cases epidemiologically connected to the Huanan Seafood Market?)

nodes.canonical: The Dalian outbreak in July 2020 occurred at a major seafood processing facility after 111 consecutive days with no local infections.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: For the Dalian outbreak in July 2020, the outbreak was in Dalian Kaiyang World Seafood, a major seafood processing facility. This was the first local outbreak reported after having no new local infections in Dalian for 111 consecutive days.

supported_by.canonical: Two out of five large COVID-19 outbreaks in China during 2020 originated in markets, along with outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore seafood markets.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: 2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020, as well as outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore, started in markets.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
Contrary to this claim, an empirical analysis reveals that seafood markets and facilities repeatedly formed initial Covid clusters following a period of zero infections. This was observed in 2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020 (Xinfadi and Dalian), as well as in outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore:

For the Xinfadi outbreak in June 2020: among the 368 persons isolated and treated, 272 (73.9%) had epidemiologic links to the market—169 (46%) were market workers and 103 (28%) were visitors.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote: Peter: The first officially confirmed COVID case was a vendor at the Wuhan wet market. So were the next four, and half of the next 40. A heat map of early cases is obviously centered on the wet market, not on the lab. The wet market and the lab are about 6 miles away as the crow flies, or a 15 mile / half hour drive.

attestations.author: Scott Alexander
attestations.quote:
A map of cases at the wet market itself shows a clear pattern in favor of the very southwest corner:




The southwest corner is where most of the wildlife was being sold.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote:
A map of cases at the wet market itself shows a clear pattern in favor of the very southwest corner:



The southwest corner is where most of the wildlife was being sold. Rumor said that included a stall with raccoon-dogs, an animal which is generally teeming with weird coronaviruses, and is a plausible intermediate host between humans and bats:

However, this interpretation is misleading as it primarily reflects increased sampling in the area where wildlife is sold.

relation.rationale: The Dalian seafood processing facility outbreak in July 2020 provides another documented Chinese outbreak that contributes to the evidence of markets forming COVID-19 clusters.

nodes.canonical: Thailand experienced an outbreak traced to a seafood market in mid-December 2020 after six months of near-zero infections, with over 1,300 cases linked to the market.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: In Thailand in mid-December 2020, after 6 months of near zero infections, more than 1,300 cases were traced to a seafood market in Samut Sakhon, a coastal province near Bangkok.

attestations.author: Jonathan (Rootclaim)
attestations.quote: Most notable among them is the December 2020 outbreak in Thailand, following a long period of zero Covid (i.e. forming an early cluster just like HSM). That too happened in a seafood market, and the index case there was also a shrimp vendor!
